1: A revelation of God: divine speech continues in Numbers 15 immediately after the judgment of chapter 14 — God begins giving new instructions for when the next generation enters the land. The word of God does not fall silent in judgment; grace and instruction persist. Christ is the living Word who never ceases to speak (Hebrews 1:2; Revelation 3:20).
2: A revelation of God: "when you come into the land you are to inhabit, which I am giving you" — the land-entrance is stated as certain even after the preceding judgment. God's promise to the second generation stands firm despite the first generation's failure. This is the pattern of divine faithfulness that Paul develops: the unbelief of some does not nullify the faithfulness of God (Romans 3:3).
3: A type: the sacrificial offerings by fire — burnt offering, vow fulfillment, freewill offering — constitute the structure of Israel's worship. Every offering is a shadow of the one full, final, and sufficient sacrifice of Christ, who offered himself as a "pleasing aroma" to God (Ephesians 5:2: "a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God").
4: A type: the grain offering of fine flour mixed with oil accompanying every sacrifice is the bread-component of the covenant meal shared with God. The Lord's Supper is the new covenant fulfillment of this meal — bread and cup shared in the presence of God as the substance of what the grain and drink offerings foreshadowed (1 Corinthians 11:23-26).
5: A type: wine as the drink of the covenant offering — a quarter hin of wine accompanying the sacrifice. Jesus at the Last Supper identifies the cup as "my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many" (Matthew 26:28), making the wine of the new covenant meal the fulfillment of every cup poured out in Israel's sacrificial worship.
6: A type: the graduated offerings — different quantities for lambs, rams, and bulls — reflect the gradations of the sacrificial system. Christ's single sacrifice supersedes all gradations: the "more perfect" sacrifice of Hebrews 9:11-12 fulfills the entire graduated system with one complete offering.
7: A type: "a pleasing aroma to the LORD" — the drink offering of wine described in terms of the sacrificial fragrance that ascends to God. Ephesians 5:2 applies this language directly to Christ: he gave himself up for us, "a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God," identifying the cross as the fulfillment of every pleasing aroma in Israel's worship.
8: A type: the young bull — the most costly animal in the sacrificial scale — accompanied by the largest grain and wine portions. Hebrews contrasts Israel's bull sacrifices with Christ's self-offering: "it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins" (Hebrews 10:4), but Christ "through his own blood entered once for all into the holy places" (Hebrews 9:12).
9: A type: three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour for the bull's grain offering — the largest portion in the table. The fullness of the grain offering accompanying the greatest sacrifice points to Christ's offering as complete and final; nothing is lacking in his self-giving (Colossians 2:9-10: "in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily").
10: A type: half a hin of wine for the bull's drink offering — the largest wine portion, a pleasing aroma. The ascending quantity of wine across the sacrifice scale (quarter hin for lamb, third hin for ram, half hin for bull) creates a table of escalating completeness that finds its terminus in Christ, who gives his blood as the one offering that establishes the new covenant (Luke 22:20).
11: A type: "this shall be done for each bull, each ram, and each lamb or goat" — the rule of repetition applies to every animal. The necessity of repeating the sacrificial rite for every offering reflects the inability of the OT system to achieve permanent atonement. Hebrews draws this contrast explicitly: "every priest stands daily at his service" because the work is never finished; "when Christ had offered for all time a single sacrifice for sins, he sat down" (Hebrews 10:11-12).
12: A type: "whatever the number of animals you offer, do this for each one according to their number." The multiplicability of the system — the same rule for one or a thousand animals — underscores its provisional character. No quantity of OT sacrifices achieves what one sacrifice of Christ accomplishes (Hebrews 9:26: "he has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself").
13: A revelation of God: "every native Israelite shall follow this procedure" — the law applies to all who belong to the covenant community. The universal scope of the law's requirement prepares for the universal scope of Christ's fulfillment: "he is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the whole world" (1 John 2:2).
14: A shadow: the resident alien (ger) who presents an offering following the same procedure as the native Israelite is incorporated into Israel's sacrificial worship. This inclusion of the stranger foreshadows the Gentile inclusion in Christ: "he has made us both one and has broken down the dividing wall of hostility" (Ephesians 2:14), making the stranger a full participant in the household of God.
15: A shadow: "there shall be one statute for the congregation — for you and for the resident alien." One law for native and stranger in the community of worship directly anticipates Paul's proclamation that in Christ "there is neither Jew nor Greek, neither slave nor free, neither male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus" (Galatians 3:28). The numerical oneness of the statute becomes the theological oneness of the body.
16: A shadow: "one law and one rule shall apply to you and to the resident alien." The unity of the law in the covenant community points to the one mediator of the new covenant: "there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus" (1 Timothy 2:5). The singularity of the rule is the singularity of the way into the Father's presence.
17: A revelation of God: divine speech to Moses continues — God teaching his people about life in the promised land before they are even there. This anticipatory instruction reveals the God who gives frameworks for flourishing before the circumstances arrive. Christ gives his disciples instructions for the mission they will carry out in his absence (Matthew 28:18-20; John 14-16).
18: A revelation of God: "when you come into the land to which I am bringing you" — the certainty of arrival expressed as the premise for instruction. God legislates for a future he has guaranteed. The God who brings his people into rest (the land) is the same God who "has caused us to be born again to a living hope" (1 Peter 1:3) for an imperishable inheritance.
19: A type: the firstfruits offering from the land's produce — presenting the first portion to the LORD. 1 Corinthians 15:20 applies the firstfruits concept directly to Christ: "Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep." Jesus is the first-fruits of the resurrection harvest; his resurrection guarantees the resurrection of all who belong to him.
20: A type: "challah" — the first of the dough presented as a loaf to the LORD. The firstfruits of the ground and the firstfruits of the dough both express the principle that the first belongs to God. Colossians 1:15-18 applies this principle to Christ as "the firstborn of all creation" and "the firstborn from the dead," the one in whom and through whom all things are presented back to the Father.
21: A type: "from the first of your dough you shall give the LORD a contribution throughout your generations." The ongoing firstfruits obligation across generations points to the permanent, once-for-all firstfruits offering of Christ's resurrection. What Israel repeated each generation, Christ fulfills once in a way that makes repetition unnecessary: he is the eternal firstfruits (Revelation 1:5: "the firstborn of the dead").
22: A type: "if you sin unintentionally and do not observe all these commandments" — the category of unintentional sin (shegagah) requiring atonement. The sacrificial system acknowledges that even sins committed in ignorance constitute a breach of covenant and require covering. Christ's atoning death covers not only deliberate sins but those committed in ignorance (Luke 23:34: "Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do").
23: A revelation of God: "all that the LORD commanded you through Moses, from the day the LORD gave the command" — the scope of the commandment covering the entire Mosaic revelation. Christ comes not to abolish this comprehensive commandment but to fulfill it (Matthew 5:17), embodying in his person the whole of what the law requires.
24: A type: if the community sins unintentionally without the congregation's knowledge, the whole congregation presents a burnt offering for the whole assembly. The principle of communal atonement for shared sin anticipates Christ's sacrifice as the atonement for the whole people: "he is the propitiation for our sins" — not for individual sins only but for the corporate failure of humanity.
25: A type: "the priest shall make atonement for the whole congregation of Israel, and they shall be forgiven." The priestly atonement achieving communal forgiveness is the OT foundation for Christ's high-priestly work. Hebrews 9:11-14 presents Christ as the high priest who enters the greater tabernacle and achieves by his own blood the "eternal redemption" that the Aaronic priests could only approximate.
26: A shadow: "the whole congregation of Israel shall be forgiven, along with the resident alien living among them, since all the people were involved unintentionally." The inclusive forgiveness — covering both native and stranger — prefigures the scope of Christ's atonement, which crosses every ethnic and social boundary: "he himself is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the whole world" (1 John 2:2).
27: A type: the individual who sins unintentionally brings a yearling female goat for a sin offering. The provision for individual sin alongside communal atonement reflects the double scope of Christ's work — his death is both for the community as a whole and for each believer individually: "the Son of God, who loved me and gave himself for me" (Galatians 2:20).
28: A type: "the priest shall make atonement before the LORD for the person who sinned unintentionally, making atonement for that person, and he shall be forgiven." The priestly mediation achieving personal forgiveness is the OT pattern that Christ fulfills as the one mediator between God and man — the high priest who not only makes atonement but is himself the atoning sacrifice (1 John 2:1-2).
29: A shadow: "you shall have one law for the person who acts unintentionally, whether native-born among the Israelites or a resident alien." The unity of the law across every social boundary anticipates the oneness of the gospel: one Lord, one faith, one baptism (Ephesians 4:5), equally available to all who approach through Christ.
30: A revelation of God: "the person who acts defiantly (beyad ramah, with a high hand) — that person blasphemes the LORD and shall be cut off." The high-handed sin — deliberate, knowing rejection of God's word — is the category for which the sacrificial system provides no atonement. Hebrews 10:26-27 draws on exactly this distinction: "if we sin deliberately after receiving the knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins, but a fearful expectation of judgment." The cross covers sins committed in weakness; persistent, knowing contempt of God's grace places itself outside the covenant.
31: A revelation of God: "that person shall be utterly cut off (hikaret tikaret); his guilt is on himself." The karet penalty — being cut off from the people — is the most severe covenant sanction short of death. Christ bears the ultimate karet on behalf of those who believe (Galatians 3:13: "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us"), so that those in him need not bear their own guilt.
32: A revelation of God: the narrative of the Sabbath wood-gatherer is the law's test case immediately following the high-handed sin law. Gathering wood on the Sabbath — a deliberate, visible act — represents the kind of knowing violation that the preceding statute addressed. The Sabbath law's severity reveals the seriousness with which God treats covenant structure.
33: A revelation of God: bringing the man to Moses, Aaron, and the whole congregation for judgment reveals the communal nature of covenant keeping. The violation of one affects the whole community. Christ's role as the one who "takes away the sin of the world" (John 1:29) addresses the social dimension of sin — its impact on the covenant community — not merely the individual's guilt.
34: A revelation of God: "they put him in custody, because it had not been made clear what should be done to him." Even in the Mosaic system, interpretive uncertainty required pausing before applying the law. This points to the need for the one who embodies and fulfills the law's intent — Christ, who does not merely apply rules but is himself the living Word who gives the law its final meaning.
35: A revelation of God: "the man shall be put to death; the whole congregation shall stone him with stones outside the camp." The death penalty for Sabbath violation reveals the weight of the covenant commitment. Galatians 3:13 explains the cross in these terms: Christ became a curse for us — bearing the death that the law pronounced on covenant violation — so that those in him are freed from the law's death sentence.
36: A type with direct NT citation: executed "outside the camp." Hebrews 13:12 draws on this precise location: "Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood." The exclusion from the sacred camp is the form that covenant judgment takes; Christ, bearing that judgment, is himself executed outside the holy city, fulfilling the spatial logic of the law's most severe penalty.
37: A revelation of God: the LORD speaks to Moses again — divine instruction continues immediately after the judicial execution. God's teaching is uninterrupted by the hard acts of covenant enforcement. The one who must say "the man shall be put to death" is the same one who immediately turns to give life-giving instructions about the tzitzit. Judgment and grace flow from the same source.
38: A type: "make tassels (tzitzit) on the corners of your garments, and put a cord of blue (tekhelet) on each tassel." The tzitzit are a visible embodiment of Torah on the body. Jesus wears this garment — he is an observant Jew who keeps the Torah — and his kraspedon (hem/tassel) becomes a point of healing: the woman with the hemorrhage touches the hem of his garment and is healed (Matthew 9:20; Numbers 14:36). The one who embodies the Torah's fullness has a garment from which healing flows.
39: A type: "the tassels shall be for you to look at, so that you may remember all the LORD's commandments and do them." The tzitzit function as a visual mnemonic for the law — an external reminder of an internal obligation. Christ is the embodiment of what the tzitzit represent: he is the living Torah (John 1:1, 14), the Word made flesh. To behold him is to behold the fullness of what God's instruction was always pointing toward.
40: A revelation of God: "so you shall remember and do all my commandments, and you shall be holy to your God." The goal of Torah-remembrance is qedushah — holiness, being set apart for God. The NT presents Christ as the one who achieves holiness for his people: "Christ Jesus, who became to us wisdom from God, righteousness and sanctification and redemption" (1 Corinthians 1:30). The holiness the tzitzit commanded, Christ provides.
41: A revelation of God: "I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God." The Exodus-identity formula grounds every commandment in the prior act of redemption — obedience follows liberation, not the reverse. This is the structure of the gospel: "We love because he first loved us" (1 John 4:19). Baptism is the NT's Exodus (1 Corinthians 10:1-2), and life in the Spirit is the NT's walking in the commandments of the God who has already redeemed his people.
